This post tells the story of how rubyfmt came to be.

title: AI for Bio has a Fuzzy API problem
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-05, Reading time: 20 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fankitg.me%2Fblog%2F2026%2F05%2F04%2Ffuzzy_api.html%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019df7ab22fc-d4d35dc2-3397-45fc-ab4d-a06b7a9e865b-000000/r6Lg1EYiMpKvYdsSVBdDXWhzihhUkKbeNAHm6K1_3XA=452
summary: AI for Bio has a Fuzzy API problem (20 minute read) Biology is a uniquely hard application domain for machine learning. Drug discovery doesn't have the kind of clean feedback loops and clean interfaces that make modern ML so powerful elsewhere. Software has clean APIs, and when things break, you can usually trace the failure to a bug and fix the code. Biology's outputs are a lot fuzzier as there are many caveats at every step of the process.

title: How Monero's proof of work works
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-05, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.alcazarsec.com%2Ftech%2Fposts%2Fhow-moneros-proof-of-work-works%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019df7d364f5-49456f60-e742-4ec8-bbd4-5b994cd94ead-000000/xJJ60Q8i6c4E_ZD2LVo_lvNLP58tnYUBJxAQPGH08Hk=452
summary: How Monero's proof of work works (11 minute read) Monero's RandomX proof-of-work algorithm is designed to promote decentralization by mimicking general-purpose CPU workloads and resisting specialized ASIC mining hardware. It does this by generating dynamic, random virtual machine programs that heavily use CPU caches, memory, and floating-point units, requiring a lot of RAM.

title: Branch‑Avoidant Programming
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-05, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Feasylang.online%2Fblog%2Fbranchless%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019df7d364f5-49456f60-e742-4ec8-bbd4-5b994cd94ead-000000/BP-mEPJQhThCmLFDZc3bmW3CVs3AmXYzvggwNODjXDk=452
summary: Branch‑Avoidant Programming (5 minute read) Avoiding costly branch mispredictions from conditional 'if' statements is critical for CPU optimization because they force the processor to stall and restart its execution pipeline. A "branchless" approach solves this by replacing conditional jumps with arithmetic operations to maintain a linear flow, potentially improving performance many times over the branched method.

title: Small language models are reshaping enterprise AI architecture
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-05, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.infoworld.com%2Farticle%2F4160404%2Fsmall-language-models-rethinking-enterprise-ai-architecture.html%3Futm_source=tldrit/1/0100019df8216f06-ad2ac6f9-aa6e-445e-8804-76431fcf13a5-000000/iAoJdXi-72e3wGrp39lKiV0bSsfYAZa8HQL2T7jaBTE=452
summary: Small language models are reshaping enterprise AI architecture (5 minute read) Enterprises are increasingly routing routine tasks to smaller, specialized models while reserving large models for complex reasoning, cutting inference costs by as much as 90%, reducing latency, and keeping sensitive data closer to home. The bigger shift is toward multi-model AI stacks where companies optimize for cost, speed, and privacy instead of defaulting every workflow to the biggest model available.

summary: The $570K canary: What AI coding agents reveal about enterprise AI's real gaps (9 minute read) Anthropic's heavy use of AI-generated code and its willingness to still pay top engineers up to $570K highlights a broader shift: engineers are moving from writing code to orchestrating AI agents. Software development is scaling AI faster than other functions because it already has what most enterprises lack: governance, observability, testing frameworks, deployment controls, and audit trails.

summary: Pipelock launches open-source firewall for AI agents (2 minute read) Pipelock is an open-source firewall that sits between AI agents and the internet to block credential leaks, prompt injection attacks, SSRF attempts, and poisoned tool responses before agents take action. title: Cognitive Surrender
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-06, Reading time: 15 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Faddyosmani.com%2Fblog%2Fcognitive-surrender%2F%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019dfccf8152-423c706e-201a-45a0-8c18-d23913a15b76-000000/IVeJGae20DGO5jPiVDtwCM5k8EbckbEynIhn2_oO5m8=452
summary: Cognitive Surrender (15 minute read) Cognitive surrender is when AI output quietly becomes your output and you feel there is nothing left to check. Cognitive offloading is delegating to AI but still owning the answer. Most software engineers move between the two, but they cross the line without noticing. They are borrowing the AI's confidence and treating it as their own.

summary: Context Mode (GitHub Repo) Context Mode is an MCP server that reduces AI coding-agent context waste by sandboxing tool output, indexing large results locally, and returning only compact, relevant summaries instead of dumping raw logs, files, or API responses into the chat. title: The real cost of React Native animations: benchmarking every approach
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-06, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fexpo.dev%2Fblog%2Fthe-real-cost-of-react-native-animations-benchmarking-every-approach%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dfcfafb19-37cf4f13-304d-4d8e-8a8c-c05cdbb95783-000000/_d_GjDDyi6VovIfj2GbFwZWimwnNbSTQnHa7UCceNK4=452
summary: The real cost of React Native animations: benchmarking every approach (11 minute read) Benchmarking various React Native animation libraries show that standard approaches often consume the frame budget through repetitive shadow tree commits, leading to performance stutters. title: Async Rust never left the MVP state
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-06, Reading time: 13 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Ftweedegolf.nl%2Fen%2Fblog%2F237%2Fasync-rust-never-left-the-mvp-state%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dfcfafb19-37cf4f13-304d-4d8e-8a8c-c05cdbb95783-000000/2hANwsc4O3GWTSvBXsyGbB3aM_jtwRTCS17bGcHrUYc=452
summary: Async Rust never left the MVP state (13 minute read) Async Rust currently introduces binary bloat on microcontrollers because compiler-generated state machines are not yet true zero-cost abstractions. A formal project goal and funding are being sought to integrate compiler optimizations that collapse identical states and remove unnecessary overhead, so that these abstractions can be brought closer to their intended efficiency.

title: Don't become a wrapper
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-06, Reading time: 2 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FLcxfv7/1/0100019dfd4877de-32ec0c5e-6b26-4c00-ac92-69b85523d906-000000/f6U3s-IifeAdGLVF0AI9TXi9G7jNr8BulRm5f-YtBqY=452
summary: Don't become a wrapper (2 minute read) It has become a trend for engineers to turn themselves into agent wrappers. They interact with models to get them to generate their work, but they don't actually make decisions or do any reasoning. It is appealing to use tools that output work good enough that the failure modes don't bite right away. People need to consider what remains defensible when anyone with the same agent can get the same outputs.

title: DuckDB Internals: Why is DuckDB Fast?
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-07, Reading time: 17 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.greybeam.ai%2Fblog%2Fduckdb-internals-part-1%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019e01e98f4d-638252b4-bb3d-4642-8dbf-318ff21fd721-000000/l9hM3Y-OylNvPxz5etmnnKuqNOeCIlUCuUOHCwCryOE=452
summary: DuckDB Internals: Why is DuckDB Fast? (17 minute read) DuckDB is fast because it runs in-process, avoids server/client data movement, and combines columnar storage, query optimization, predicate pushdown, vectorized execution, and row-group pruning to scan only the data it needs. This post explains how DuckDB turns SQL into an executable plan and why its storage and Parquet-reading model make analytics feel unusually fast on a single machine.

This modern syntax avoids these collision issues, expresses intent more clearly, collapses two-sided ranges into a single expression, and is fully compatible with @container queries.

title: Your AI Agent Will Eventually Delete Prod
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-07, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fdev.to%2Fpat9000%2Fyour-ai-agent-will-eventually-delete-prod-3k4a%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019e0220d9ed-f77e604a-40f7-4468-9acf-0244675eb34e-000000/KBuiq8p_KAVAxvf7vT4_hEdd4MlO8B3HR5m5EEh--NE=452
summary: Your AI Agent Will Eventually Delete Prod (4 minute read) A Cursor agent told to clean up unused files at PocketOS interpreted the scope too widely and destroyed both the production database and the backups, which lived on the same host. The fix is layered defense, such as minimum-credential agents, isolated backups, PR-gated schema changes, dependency scanning, runtime spend rails, and human confirmation on destructive commands.
